prompt: Roughly how many babies are born worldwide each day?
note: 8 billion people, born at ~16 per 1,000 a year -> ~130 million/year / 365 ~ 360,000 a day. (UN: ~363,000.)

prompt: Roughly how many breaths does a person take in a day?
note: You breathe ~12-16 times a minute at rest. 14 x 60 x 24 ~ 20,000 — from the rate, not the figure.

prompt: How long is a marathon, in metres?
note: ~26 miles x ~1,600 m ~ 42,000 m. Fixed at 42,195 m in 1921 from the 1908 London course.

prompt: How many litres of water fill an Olympic-size swimming pool?
note: 50 m x 25 m x 2 m = 2,500 m^3, and 1 m^3 = 1,000 L -> 2,500,000 L. Pure geometry.

prompt: What is the average distance from the Earth to the Moon, in kilometres?
note: About 384,400 km on average — roughly 30 Earth-diameters of empty space between us.

prompt: What is the average distance from the Earth to the Sun, in kilometres?
note: About 149.6 million km — this distance is defined as one Astronomical Unit (AU).

prompt: Roughly how many litres of blood does the heart pump in a day?
note: ~70 mL per beat x ~100,000 beats a day ~ 7,000 litres pumped daily.

prompt: How tall is the Burj Khalifa, in metres?
note: 828 m to the tip — held the 'world's tallest' title since 2010.

prompt: How tall would a stack of one million sheets of paper be, in metres?
note: A sheet is ~0.1 mm thick. 1,000,000 x 0.1 mm = 100,000 mm = 100 m.

prompt: How long is the River Nile, in kilometres?
note: About 6,650 km — long considered the world's longest river, though the Amazon is a close rival.

prompt: Counting one number per second nonstop, how many days to reach a million?
note: 1,000,000 / 86,400 seconds a day ~ 11.6 days of nonstop counting.

prompt: Roughly how many times does an adult heart beat in a day?
note: ~70 beats a minute x 60 x 24 ~ 100,000 a day. Derive it from the rate.

prompt: How fast does sound travel through air, in metres per second?
note: About 343 m/s at 20 C — which is why you see lightning before you hear thunder.

prompt: Roughly how many strides does a runner take to finish a marathon?
note: A running stride is ~1.4 m. 42,195 m / 1.4 ~ 30,000 strides.

prompt: What is the land area of Russia, in square kilometres?
note: About 17.1 million km^2 — the largest country on Earth, nearly twice the size of Canada.

prompt: What is the diameter of the Earth, in kilometres?
note: About 12,742 km on average — slightly wider at the equator than pole-to-pole.

prompt: Roughly how far does a typical person walk in a lifetime, in kilometres?
note: ~4,000 steps/day x ~0.75 m x 365 x ~80 years ~ 90,000 km — more than twice around the Earth.

prompt: How many hours are there in a (non-leap) year?
How deep is the deepest point of the ocean (the Mariana Trench), in metres?
note: About 10,935 m at Challenger Deep — deeper than Everest is tall.

prompt: What is the diameter of the Sun, in kilometres?
note: About 1.39 million km — roughly 109 Earths laid side by side.

prompt: How many minutes are there in a week?
note: 60 x 24 x 7 = 10,080 minutes.

prompt: Roughly how many leaves are on a large mature tree?
note: ~200 leafy twigs x ~1,000 leaves each ~ 200,000 on a mature tree.

prompt: How long is the Amazon River, in kilometres?
note: About 6,400 km — its exact length is debated, with some surveys claiming it beats the Nile.

prompt: How long is the Great Barrier Reef, in kilometres?
note: About 2,300 km — the largest living structure on Earth, visible from space.

prompt: Roughly how many litres of water does a person drink in a year?
note: ~2 litres a day x 365 ~ 730 litres a year.

prompt: How fast does the Earth travel around the Sun, in kilometres per second?
note: About 30 km/s — you're hurtling through space far faster than any rocket while sitting still.

prompt: Roughly how many words does the average person speak in a day?
note: ~125 words/min for ~2 hours of talking a day ~ 15,000. A landmark study put it near 16,000.

prompt: Roughly how many steps does an average person walk in a year?
note: ~4,000 steps a day for most people x 365 ~ 1.5 million a year.

prompt: How many minutes are there in a day?
note: 60 x 24 = 1,440 minutes.

prompt: How many times does a car tyre rotate over a 100 km drive?
note: A tyre is ~0.66 m across, so ~2.1 m around. 100,000 m / 2.1 ~ 48,000 turns.

prompt: Roughly how many hours does a person sleep in a year?
note: ~8 hours a night x 365 ~ 2,920 hours — about a third of the year.

prompt: Roughly how many grains of rice are in a 1 kg bag?
note: One grain weighs ~0.022 g. 1,000 g / 0.022 ~ 45,000 grains.

prompt: Roughly how many words are in a typical novel?
note: ~300 words a page x ~300 pages ~ 90,000 words.

prompt: What is the straight-line distance from London to New York, in kilometres?
note: About 5,570 km as the crow flies — actual flight paths curve north over the Atlantic.

prompt: How many seconds are there in a week?
How many countries are there in Africa?
note: 54 — the most of any continent. People lowball it; Africa has more countries than Europe.

prompt: What is the diameter of the Moon, in kilometres?
note: About 3,474 km — roughly a quarter of Earth's diameter, or the width of Australia.

prompt: How many chromosomes are in a typical human cell?
note: 46 — that's 23 pairs, one set from each parent.

prompt: Roughly how many languages are spoken in the world today?
note: About 7,000 — though nearly half have fewer than 10,000 speakers and are at risk of vanishing.

prompt: How many seconds are there in a (non-leap) year?
note: 31,536,000 — handy rule of thumb: a year is about pi times 10 million seconds.

prompt: How old is the universe, in years?
note: About 13.8 billion years — roughly three times older than the Earth.

prompt: How tall is the Eiffel Tower, in metres?
note: About 330 m to the tip — it grows a few centimetres in summer as the iron expands.

prompt: Roughly how many hairs are on a typical human head?
note: Around 100,000 — and shedding 50 to 100 a day is completely normal.

prompt: Roughly how many words are in the entire Harry Potter book series?
note: About 1.08 million across all seven books — Order of the Phoenix alone is over 250,000.

prompt: What is the area of the Sahara Desert, in square kilometres?
note: About 9.2 million km2 — nearly the size of the entire United States.

prompt: What is the circumference of the Earth around the equator, in kilometres?
note: About 40,075 km — close to a round 40,000 by design; the metre was once defined from it.

prompt: Roughly how many cells are in the human body?
note: About 37 trillion — and you're carrying a comparable number of microbes on top of that.

prompt: What is the boiling point of water at sea level, in degrees Fahrenheit?
note: 212 degF (100 degC). Fahrenheit set 0 at a freezing brine and 32 at water's freezing point.

prompt: How high is a basketball hoop above the floor, in centimetres?
note: 305 cm — exactly 10 feet, unchanged since James Naismith nailed up a peach basket in 1891.

prompt: Roughly how many islands make up Indonesia?
note: About 17,000 — the world's largest archipelago, though only a few thousand are inhabited.

prompt: What is the surface temperature of the Sun, in degrees Celsius?
note: About 5,500 degC at the surface — but the core runs to roughly 15 million.

prompt: How many teeth does a typical adult human have?
note: 32, including the four wisdom teeth. Children have 20 baby teeth.

prompt: Roughly how many neurons are in the human brain?
note: About 86 billion — the old '100 billion' figure was an estimate that got refined downward.

prompt: How long is the Trans-Siberian Railway, in kilometres?
note: 9,289 km from Moscow to Vladivostok — the world's longest railway, crossing eight time zones.

prompt: How many stitches are on a regulation baseball?
note: 108 hand-sewn double stitches — the red thread is what makes a curveball curve.

prompt: How many hours are there in a 30-day month?
note: 720 hours. Easy anchor: a day is 24, so a month is roughly 24 times 30.

prompt: How long can a blue whale grow, in metres?
note: Up to about 30 m — the largest animal ever known to have lived, longer than a basketball court.

prompt: How many individual frames are shown in a 2-hour film at 24 frames per second?
note: 172,800 frames — 24 still images flashed every second is enough to fool the eye into motion.

prompt: How many member countries does the European Union have?
note: 27, down from 28 after the UK left in 2020.

prompt: What is normal atmospheric pressure at sea level, in kilopascals?
note: About 101 kPa — the weight of the whole atmosphere pressing on every square metre.

prompt: What is normal human body temperature, in degrees Fahrenheit?
note: 98.6 degF (37 degC) is the classic figure, though normal ranges a little either side.

prompt: How long does light from the Sun take to reach Earth, in seconds?
note: About 500 seconds — roughly 8 minutes 20 seconds. You always see the Sun as it was minutes ago.

prompt: How tall is the world's tallest known tree, a coast redwood, in metres?
note: About 116 m (Hyperion) — taller than the Statue of Liberty, hidden in a California forest.

prompt: Roughly how many years ago was the Great Pyramid of Giza built?
note: About 4,500 years ago — it was already ancient when Cleopatra was alive.

prompt: At 120 beats per minute, how many beats are in a 3-minute song?
note: 360 beats. 120 bpm is a common pop tempo — about two beats every second.

prompt: How fast does a commercial jet typically cruise, in kilometres per hour?
note: Around 900 km/h — just under the speed of sound, which is why planes rarely fly faster.

prompt: Roughly how many taste buds are on the human tongue?
note: About 10,000 — and they get replaced every week or two, which is why a burnt tongue heals fast.

prompt: How many zeros are in one trillion (short scale)?
note: 12. A thousand has 3, a million 6, a billion 9 — three more each step up.

prompt: How tall is Angel Falls, the world's tallest waterfall, in metres?
note: 979 m in Venezuela — so tall that in dry months the water evaporates into mist before it lands.

prompt: What is the highest air temperature ever reliably recorded on Earth, in degrees Celsius?
note: 56.7 degC, set in Death Valley in 1913 — still the official record.
